 to do with news. And yet, those same streamers once claimed to have no int=
erest in live sports, then they went and got in bed with the NFL (and NBA, =
and MLB, and MLS).

Over the summer, Williams=E2=80=99 agents at CAA floated the idea to Netfli=
x=E2=80=99s Ted Sarandos, who predictably expressed zero interest in news o=
r news-adjacent programming. They then went to Amazon Prime Video chief Mik=
e Hopkins and his V.P. Albert Cheng, who seemed to be more intrigued by the=
 idea. In early August, Williams and Wald flew to Los Angeles to meet with =
the two executives at Amazon=E2=80=99s new state-of-the-art virtual soundst=
age in Culver City, which is home to the largest LED wall stage in the U.S.=
, and convinced them that the new venue could yield all sorts of programmin=
g innovations, including their Election Night special. Hopkins and Cheng st=
ressed the importance of a broadcast that was
politically down the middle=E2=80=94easy enough for an institutionalist lik=
e Williams=E2=80=94and Amazon and CAA got to work negotiating the terms of =
the deal.

As I reported ( https://x.com/DylanByers/status/1840006044891726010?utm_cam=
paign=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2810%2F2%2F24%29&utm_content=3DIn+The+Ro=
om+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2810%2F2%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dc=
ustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) Saturday, that deal is now effec=
tively done. On November 5, Williams will anchor the Election Night special=
 he and Wald first conceived of some seven months earlier. I can also now r=
eport that they=E2=80=99ve enlisted Glenn Weiss and Ricky Kirshner, the Emm=
y-winning production duo behind myriad Oscars and Tonys ceremonies, Super B=
owl halftime shows, Kennedy Center Honors, and presidential conventions and=
 inaugurations. They will help produce the show with Wald, who will serve a=
s executive producer and showrunner. I=E2=80=99m also told that Williams an=
d Wald have commitments
from some notable political commentators, including veteran Democratic stra=
tegist James Carville, who will join Williams=E2=80=99 broadcast exclusivel=
y, forgoing any invitations from his usual suitors at CNN and NBC.

Naturally, the Williams Election Night special has ignited speculation abou=
t Amazon=E2=80=99s ambitions in live news. At present, executives there are=
 stressing that this is merely a one-off, with no newsroom infrastructure, =
no decision desk, etcetera. And, indeed, it may be a one-night-only affair.

But it=E2=80=99s also not hard to see this as a penny ante that allows Amaz=
on, and other streamers, to consider future innovations in the news space. =
It=E2=80=99s also hard to ignore that the moment the broadcast begins, it w=
ill be available to an audience of some 200 million Amazon subscribers glob=
ally=E2=80=94more, in fact, since the special will not be behind the paywal=
l.

You can=E2=80=99t blame the economy=E2=80=99s largest technology companies =
for laughing at the
investment that media businesses have made in news-gathering=E2=80=94the ca=
pex, the sunk costs, unions, security, newsrooms, etcetera. But you can als=
o see the appeal in reshaping the molecular unit of the industry. Amazon, f=
or their part, doesn=E2=80=99t want to create a news business. Instead, the=
y want to stand up programming for their audience on high-density news nigh=
ts. And Williams seems like he=E2=80=99s game to take the risk for his thir=
d act.

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px;">
           <p style=3D"margin: 0; color: #000000; font-size: 16px; line-hei=
ght: 30px; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 0;">Greetings from Los Angeles,=
 and welcome back to In the Room, my twice-weekly private email on media an=
d media <i>people</i>. If you missed last night=E2=80=99s <b>Walz</b>-<b>Va=
nce </b>dance on CBS=E2=80=94a paradigm of Midwestern civility, like <b>Nor=
m Gunderson </b>and <b>Jerry Lundegaard </b>running for the local school bo=
ard=E2=80=94don=E2=80=99t worry, we=E2=80=99ve got you covered. In the wee =
small hours, Puck superfriends <b>John Heilemann</b>, <b>Peter Hamby</b>, a=
nd yours truly reunited again to debrief on the highlights and lowlights, a=
nd all their implications (or, as is inevitably the case in a V.P. debate, =
<i>non</i>implications). <u><a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFp=
bF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dTVF8tMFJVeW5aYWlqZTJ4ZFM1cz0iLCJocmVmIjo=
iaHR0cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5ld3MvcG9kY2FzdF9lcGlzb2RlL2NocmlzdC1oYXZlLW1lcmN5LXB1Y2=
stc3VwZXJmcmllbmRzLWNob3AtdXAtdGhlLXZwLWRlYmF0ZS8_dXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPUluK1RoZ=
StSb29tKy0rU1VCU0NSSUJFUlMrJTI4MTAlMkYyJTJGMjQlMjlcdTAwMjZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1J=
bitUaGUrUm9vbSstK1NVQlNDUklCRVJTKyUyODEwJTJGMiUyRjI0JTI5XHUwMDI2dXRtX21lZGl=
1bT1lbWFpbF9hY3Rpb25cdTAwMjZ1dG1fc291cmNlPWN1c3RvbWVyLmlvXHUwMDI2dXRtX3Rlcm=
09ZjZjNjA2MTdkYTQyYWJkMTJhIiwiaW50ZXJuYWwiOiJmNmM2MDYxN2RhNDJhYmQxMmEiLCJsa=
W5rX2lkIjoxMTIzNjU2fQ/b124d96bd0bbc8d416d4075d4c5fbe75120b8032c8eaf3ec578ee=
e7ac319c271" style=3D"color:#d40000;text-decoration:none;font-weight:bold" =
class=3D"sdf">Listen here</a></u>. <br/><br/>In tonight=E2=80=99s email, th=
e official<i> in the room </i>and <i>at the table </i>backstory of how <b>B=
rian Williams</b> and <b>Jonathan Wald </b>hatched their Amazon election sp=
ecial over chicken parm at Elio=E2=80=99s. Plus, some big scoops on who <i>=
else</i> will be participating in this first-of-its-kind broadcast, and wha=
t it may portend for the future of news on streaming.<br/><br/><i>Also ment=
ioned in this email</i>: <b>Olivia Nuzzi</b> and <b>Ryan Lizza</b>, <b>Ted =
Sarandos</b>, <b>Mark Thompson</b>,<b> Mike Hopkins </b>and <b>Albert Cheng=
</b>, <b>Hoda Kotb</b>, <b>Glenn Weiss</b>, <b>James Carville</b>, <b>Taylo=
r Lorenz</b>, <b>Andy Lack</b>, <b>Don Lemon</b>, <b>Chris Licht</b>, <b>Ce=
sar Conde</b>, <b>Kara Swisher</b>, and <b>Bruce Springsteen</b>.<br/><br/>=
<i>But first</i>=E2=80=A6<br/><br/></p><ul style=3D"margin:0;color:#000000;=
font-size:16px;line-height:30px;margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;list-style-typ=
e:square;mso-special-format:bullet"><li><i>The Nuzzi doozy</i>: The sad, pa=
thetic, creepy, completely mesmerizing <b>Olivia Nuzzi</b>-<b>Ryan Lizza </=
b>sob story took yet another sordid turn this week after the <i>New York </=
i>political journalist filed a temporary protection order against her ex-fi=
anc=C3=A9 in which she accused him of harassment, blackmail, and an attempt=
 to =E2=80=9Cdestroy=E2=80=9D her =E2=80=9Clife, career, and reputation=E2=
=80=9D by publicizing personal information. In brief, Nuzzi alleges that Li=
zza stole one of her devices and hacked into others to surveil her, then al=
erted <i>New Yor</i>k to =E2=80=9Cdamaging information=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94the=
 =E2=80=9Cdemure=E2=80=9D nudes she sent to <b>R.F.K. Jr</b>., presumably=
=E2=80=94that she claims may have been doctored. In a statement, Lizza =E2=
=80=9Cemphatically=E2=80=9D denied those allegations and said he will defen=
d himself against them =E2=80=9Cvigorously and successfully,=E2=80=9D while
noting, =E2=80=9CI am saddened that my ex-fianc=C3=A9e would resort to maki=
ng a series of false accusations against me as a way to divert attention fr=
om her own personal and professional failings.=E2=80=9D <br/><br/>The Super=
ior Court of D.C. granted Nuzzi=E2=80=99s protective order on Tuesday, orde=
red Lizza to =E2=80=9Cimmediately cease using any illegally obtained inform=
ation,=E2=80=9D and granted Nuzzi=E2=80=99s request to have police officers=
 accompany her when she goes to retrieve her belongings from Lizza. Meanwhi=
le, Lizza has taken a leave of absence from Politico while they conduct the=
ir own internal investigation. (Inarguably, he too has a conflict of intere=
st here, and probably has for some time=E2=80=94and he, <i>too</i>, should =
have told his bosses sooner if he had an inkling that he was covering a can=
didate who was engaged in a digital relationship with his girlfriend.) Nuzz=
i has also filed her accusations with the F.B.I., though they have not comm=
ented on the matter.<br/><br/>As I first reported back when
this scandal broke, Nuzzi=E2=80=99s camp started quietly advancing these ac=
cusations against Lizza the moment the news of her Kennedy <i>situationship=
</i> became public. As I noted then, there are certain things only certain =
people know for sure=E2=80=94though, presumably, these formal investigation=
s will provide greater clarity and expose at least one of these two journal=
ists as a demonstrable liar. <br/><br/>Outside the courts, Nuzzi remains th=
e subject of <i>New York</i>=E2=80=99s own internal investigation, which wi=
ll determine whether she gets to keep her job after lying to her editors ab=
out the Kennedy matter and continuing to cover a campaign in which she was =
obviously emotionally invested. That investigation is ongoing, though it se=
ems increasingly likely that <i>New York </i>and its parentco, Vox Media, m=
ay simply be going through the motions. In any event, the blast radius from=
 this whole saga continues to expand outward, with no end in sight.</li><br=
/><li><i>Some personal news
(Taylor=E2=80=99s version)</i>: Elsewhere in the nation=E2=80=99s capital, =
<i>The Washington Post</i> has severed ties with <b>Taylor Lorenz </b>after=
 determining that she misled them about an Instagram post on her account in=
 which <b>President</b> <b>Biden </b>was labeled as =E2=80=9Ca war criminal=
=2E=E2=80=9D The <i>Post</i> conducted a r=
eview of the matter and, though its=
 findings have not been made public, I can confirm that the paper determine=
d Lorenz violated the paper=E2=80=99s standards and had no desire to contin=
ue employing her. In a statement, the <i>Post</i> very delicately stated th=
at Lorenz had =E2=80=9Cresigned to pursue a career in independent journalis=
m.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>This is <i>not</i> Taylor=E2=80=99s version, of course=
=2E On Tuesday, Lorenz announced that s=
he was leaving the <i>Post</i> to laun=
ch her own substack, User Mag, where she would cover technology =E2=80=9Cfr=
om the user side=E2=80=9D and examine power dynamics on the internet. =E2=
=80=9CI just wanted to get out of legacy media,=E2=80=9D Lorenz told <i>The=
 Hollywood
Reporter</i>, which accepted that narrative. In a statement to me, Lorenz c=
alled my framing of her exit =E2=80=9Can extremely stupid attempt to manufa=
cture controversy.=E2=80=9D She added: =E2=80=9CA big part of why I decided=
 to go independent and found User Mag is so that I can speak directly for m=
yself and not have to deal with bad-faith anonymous =E2=80=98sources=E2=80=
=99 trying to smear me in the media.=E2=80=9D </li></ul><br/><br/><ul style=
=3D"margin:0;color:#000000;font-size:16px;line-height:30px;margin-top:0;mar=
gin-bottom:0;list-style-type:square;mso-special-format:bullet"><li><i>Mark =
Thompson=E2=80=99s magic wall</i>: CNN has finally launched its paywall, ch=
arging users $3.99 a month after they read a certain number of free article=
s. (Reuters also launched a paywall this week, at $1 a week.) In addition t=
o unlimited access to the site, CNN Digital chief <b>Alex MacCallum</b> sai=
d subscribers would get access to =E2=80=9Cexclusive election features, ori=
ginal documentaries, a curated daily selection of our most
distinctive journalism, and fewer digital ads.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>This is a =
step in the right direction, though one hopes it=E2=80=99s not too little, =
too late. (Alas, it almost certainly is=E2=80=A6) The product MacCallum des=
cribes sure feels <i>a lot</i> like the consumer subscription product she a=
nd her former colleagues had tried to create nearly three years ago with CN=
N+. Of course, the advantage of doing that <i>then</i> was that you could b=
uild the nascent subscription business <i>alongside</i> a still-robust line=
ar product. Now, CNN is having to wind down the legacy asset to build the n=
ew business. Tall order.</li></ul>
          </td>
         </tr>

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Earlier this year, =
on a brisk February night in New York=E2=80=94<i>so </i>many eons of media =
cycles ago=E2=80=94I found myself sitting down to dinner at Elio=E2=80=99s,=
 the swell and storied Upper East Side red sauce refuge, with <b>Brian Will=
iams</b>. By then, the well-kept newsman had been in the wilderness for mor=
e than two years: idling around his beach house in Bay Head, volunteering a=
t the nearby fire station, traveling with <b>Springsteen</b>, and entertain=
ing advances from friends and strangers who wanted to know when his familia=
r visage might grace their television screens again. That night, the entrea=
ty came first from the bartender, then from a nearby couple. And, in truth,=
 that=E2=80=99s why <i>I </i>was there, too. When Williams signed off from =
MSNBC=E2=80=99s <i>11th Hour</i>, in late 2021, he=E2=80=99d promised =E2=
=80=9Cto pop up again somewhere.=E2=80=9D <i>Where</i>, I wanted to
know, and <i>when</i>?<br/><br/>Indeed, Williams=E2=80=99 third act has bee=
n an <u><a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lE=
QUt2UktxclJLZ0dTVF8tMFJVeW5aYWlqZTJ4ZFM1cz0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5=
ld3MvdGhlLWJyaWFuLXdpbGxpYW1zLWd1ZXNzaW5nLWdhbWUtYmVnaW5zLz91dG1fY2FtcGFpZ2=
49SW4rVGhlK1Jvb20rLStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgxMCUyRjIlMkYyNCUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9jb=
250ZW50PUluK1RoZStSb29tKy0rU1VCU0NSSUJFUlMrJTI4MTAlMkYyJTJGMjQlMjlcdTAwMjZ1=
dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsX2FjdGlvblx1MDAyNnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9Y3VzdG9tZXIuaW9cdTAwMjZ=
1dG1fdGVybT1mNmM2MDYxN2RhNDJhYmQxMmEiLCJpbnRlcm5hbCI6ImY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZD=
EyYSIsImxpbmtfaWQiOjQwMjN9/f25b1bbac9f5bda1d480556147862856e6b016afed9a5f45=
f2d001972d2e758e" style=3D"color:#d40000;text-decoration:none;font-weight:b=
old" class=3D"sdf">evergreen preoccupation</a></u> among media insiders ami=
d the industry=E2=80=99s broader transformation. In many ways, Williams was=
 the nation=E2=80=99s last <i>truly</i> iconic news anchor: a natural broad=
cast talent, <b>Brokaw</b> disciple, and self-described institutionalist wh=
ose Brahminesque baritone embodied <i>the</i> <i>news</i> much the same way=
 <b>Al Michaels</b>=E2=80=99 outerborough nobility embodied the game of foo=
tball. (I grew up in an NBC household, obviously.) After being driven from =
the <i>Nightly News</i> due to his helicopter embellishment scandal=E2=80=
=94humiliating then, positively quaint now=E2=80=94Williams stuck it out at=

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Shortly after arriv=
ing at Elio=E2=80=99s, Williams and I were joined by <b>Jonathan Wald</b>, =
the veteran news producer who had overseen Williams=E2=80=99 <i>11th Hour</=
i> while serving as MSNBC=E2=80=99s programming and development chief. A fo=
rmer E.P. of the <i>Today </i>show, Wald=E2=80=99s own three-decade career =
overindexed on marquee talents who would be forced to navigate life after b=
roadcast, and who have since come to rely on his counsel. Before MSNBC, Wal=
d had worked at <b>Jeff Zucker</b>=E2=80=99s CNN as Lemon=E2=80=99s E.P.=E2=
=80=94after our dinner, Wald ventured north to watch Lemon interview <b>Kar=
a Swisher </b>at the 92nd Street Y=E2=80=94and, before that, as E.P. for <b=
>Piers Morgan</b>. Wald left NBC shortly after Williams, and the two inevit=
ably spent a fair amount of their free time batting around ideas for Willia=
ms=E2=80=99 third act, none of which ever stuck. (I later learned that
Wald had tried to encourage Williams to leave NBC earlier, in the summer of=
 2020, to seek a deal with Netflix or HBO. Alas.)<br/><br/>Over the famousl=
y off-menu chicken parm, Williams, Wald, and I discussed the pitiable state=
 of television news, its inexorable decline, and inevitable economic realig=
nment. Would CNN=E2=80=99s highly anticipated pivot to digital be able to s=
ustain all those afternoon and primetime anchors making <i>millions </i>to =
service an average audience of just <i>hundreds of thousands</i>? Inevitabl=
y not. And yet, how could a network like CNN or NBC be expected to program =
breaking news events or big political nights <i>without </i>those talents? =
<br/><br/>Wald proffered an unorthodox idea: What if you paid the <b>Anders=
on Cooper</b>s and <b>Jake Tapper</b>s <i>less</i> money to <i>work</i> les=
s, freeing them up to pursue extracurricular projects while keeping them on=
 hand for the <i>big </i>news nights, like an election, that really mattere=
d to the brand and
business. It was imperfect, Wald conceded, perhaps implausible, but not tot=
ally irrational. After all, MSNBC had just paid <b>Maddow </b>to work less,=
 on Mondays and during the big news events. (Sure, they=E2=80=99d paid her =
<i>more </i>to work less, but whatever.)<br/><br/>Suddenly, Wald lit upon a=
nother idea. =E2=80=9CYou could do that,=E2=80=9D he said to Williams. =E2=
=80=9C<i>Election Night in America With Brian Williams</i>. One night only.=
=E2=80=9D It would require some investment, sure, but they wouldn=E2=80=99t=
 need to stand up a newsroom or any newsgathering infrastructure. They coul=
d simply put Williams behind a desk in a studio, enlist a small team of exp=
ert analysts who weren=E2=80=99t already tethered to contributor contracts =
elsewhere, program around the returns coming out of other networks=E2=80=94=
almost like an NFL RedZone for Election Night=E2=80=94and distribute it thr=
ough a streamer. Williams=E2=80=99 interest was piqued by the idea; if memo=
ry serves, he likened it to <b>Sinatra</b>=E2=80=99s one-night-only event a=
t the
Garden.<br/></td>

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Fittingly, many of =
Williams=E2=80=99 most pivotal career moves have been hatched over white-ta=
blecloth Italian meals=E2=80=94indeed, <b>Andy Lack</b> first <u><a href=3D=
"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dTVF=
8tMFJVeW5aYWlqZTJ4ZFM1cz0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5ld3MvdGhlLWJyaWFuL=
XdpbGxpYW1zLWd1ZXNzaW5nLWdhbWUtYmVnaW5zLz91dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249SW4rVGhlK1Jvb20r=
LStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgxMCUyRjIlMkYyNCUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9jb250ZW50PUluK1RoZSt=
Sb29tKy0rU1VCU0NSSUJFUlMrJTI4MTAlMkYyJTJGMjQlMjlcdTAwMjZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYW=
lsX2FjdGlvblx1MDAyNnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9Y3VzdG9tZXIuaW9cdTAwMjZ1dG1fdGVybT1mNmM2M=
DYxN2RhNDJhYmQxMmEiLCJpbnRlcm5hbCI6ImY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZDEyYSIsImxpbmtfaWQi=
OjQwMjN9/f25b1bbac9f5bda1d480556147862856e6b016afed9a5f45f2d001972d2e758e" =
style=3D"color:#d40000;text-decoration:none;font-weight:bold" class=3D"sdf"=
>pitched him</a></u> on the post-Choppergate MSNBC redemption effort some 2=
4 blocks south, at Canaletto. And yet, Wald=E2=80=99s idea seemed to hinge =
on the uncertain premise that streamers like Netflix and Amazon and Apple a=
ctually had <i>any </i>interest in live news. Wald acknowledged as much: In=
 order for a streamer to want it, he said, they would have to embrace the i=
dea as their own and be willing to withstand the slings and arrows that com=
e with live news: the
uncertainty, the scrutiny, etcetera. To date, streamers have wanted <i>noth=
ing </i>to do with news. And yet, those same streamers once claimed to have=
 no interest in live sports, then they went and got in bed with the NFL (an=
d NBA, and MLB, and MLS).<br/><br/>Over the summer, Williams=E2=80=99 agent=
s at CAA floated the idea to Netflix=E2=80=99s <b>Ted Sarandos</b>, who pre=
dictably expressed zero interest in news or news-adjacent programming. They=
 then went to Amazon Prime Video chief <b>Mike Hopkins </b>and his V.P. <b>=
Albert Cheng</b>, who seemed to be more intrigued by the idea. In early Aug=
ust, Williams and Wald flew to Los Angeles to meet with the two executives =
at Amazon=E2=80=99s new state-of-the-art virtual soundstage in Culver City,=
 which is home to the largest LED wall stage in the U.S., and convinced the=
m that the new venue could yield all sorts of programming innovations, incl=
uding their Election Night special. Hopkins and Cheng stressed the importan=
ce of a broadcast that was
politically down the middle=E2=80=94easy enough for an institutionalist lik=
e Williams=E2=80=94and Amazon and CAA got to work negotiating the terms of =
the deal.<br/><br/>As I <u><a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpb=
F9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dTVF8tMFJVeW5aYWlqZTJ4ZFM1cz0iLCJocmVmIjoi=
aHR0cHM6Ly94LmNvbS9EeWxhbkJ5ZXJzL3N0YXR1cy8xODQwMDA2MDQ0ODkxNzI2MDEwP3V0bV9=
jYW1wYWlnbj1JbitUaGUrUm9vbSstK1NVQlNDUklCRVJTKyUyODEwJTJGMiUyRjI0JTI5XHUwMD=
I2dXRtX2NvbnRlbnQ9SW4rVGhlK1Jvb20rLStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgxMCUyRjIlMkYyNCUyO=
Vx1MDAyNnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWxfYWN0aW9uXHUwMDI2dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1jdXN0b21lci5p=
b1x1MDAyNnV0bV90ZXJtPWY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZDEyYSIsImludGVybmFsIjoiZjZjNjA2MTd=
kYTQyYWJkMTJhIiwibGlua19pZCI6MTEyMzcyOX0/bbe17473f585f7e2cb5f78559fc9d6a544=
c7390559e7c6c3738b20171e713e0a" style=3D"color:#d40000;text-decoration:none=
;font-weight:bold" class=3D"sdf">reported</a></u> Saturday, that deal is no=
w effectively done. On November 5, Williams will anchor the Election Night =
special he and Wald first conceived of some seven months earlier. I can als=
o now report that they=E2=80=99ve enlisted <b>Glenn Weiss </b>and <b>Ricky =
Kirshner</b>, the Emmy-winning production duo behind myriad Oscars and Tony=
s ceremonies, Super Bowl halftime shows, Kennedy Center Honors, and preside=
ntial conventions and inaugurations. They will help produce the show with W=
ald, who will serve as executive producer and showrunner. I=E2=80=99m also =
told that Williams and Wald have commitments
from some notable political commentators, including veteran Democratic stra=
tegist <b>James Carville</b>, who will join Williams=E2=80=99 broadcast exc=
lusively, forgoing any invitations from his usual suitors at CNN and NBC. <=
br/><br/>Naturally, the Williams Election Night special has ignited specula=
tion about Amazon=E2=80=99s ambitions in live news. At present, executives =
there are stressing that this is merely a one-off, with no newsroom infrast=
ructure, no decision desk, etcetera. And, indeed, it may be a one-night-onl=
y affair.<br/><br/>But it=E2=80=99s also not hard to see this as a penny an=
te that allows Amazon, and other streamers, to consider future innovations =
in the news space. It=E2=80=99s also hard to ignore that the moment the bro=
adcast begins, it will be available to an audience of some 200 million Amaz=
on subscribers globally=E2=80=94more, in fact, since the special will not b=
e behind the paywall. <br/><br/>You can=E2=80=99t blame the economy=E2=80=
=99s largest technology companies for laughing at the
investment that media businesses have made in news-gathering=E2=80=94the ca=
pex, the sunk costs, unions, security, newsrooms, etcetera. But you can als=
o see the appeal in reshaping the molecular unit of the industry. Amazon, f=
or their part, doesn=E2=80=99t want to create a news business. Instead, the=
y want to stand up programming for their audience on high-density news nigh=
ts. And Williams seems like he=E2=80=99s game to take the risk for his thir=
d act. <br/></td>
